method3.generated_article:
==Etymology==
The name *Hawaii* is generally thought to derive from the Proto-Polynesian term *hawaiki*, the ancestral homeland of Polynesians.[1][2] Some sources gloss the term as meaning “Place of the Gods,” while others connect it with Raiatea and Mauna Kea and Mauna Loa in traditional interpretations.[2] The spelling *Hawai‘i* reflects the Hawaiian-language form of the name.[1][2]

==Geography and environment==
Hawaii is a chain of volcanic islands in the central Pacific Ocean consisting of eight major islands and 124 minor islands, with a total land area of 6,425 square miles and a general coastline of about 750 miles. [2][5] The major islands, from west to east, are Niihau, Kauai, Oahu, Molokai, Lanai, Kahoolawe, Maui, and Hawaii, and the state stretches in a broad northwest-southeast arc across more than 1,500 miles of ocean. [5] Honolulu is 214 miles from Hilo, 1,367 miles from Kure Atoll, 2,397 miles from San Francisco, and 4,829 miles from Washington, D.C. [2][4]

The state’s highest peak is Mauna Kea, which rises 13,796 feet above sea level. [2][4] Other notable physical features include Kaukonahua Stream on Oahu, the state’s longest stream at 33 miles, Halalii on Niihau, the largest lake at 841 acres, and Kahiwa Falls on Molokai, the highest named waterfall at 1,750 feet. [2][4] Hawaii also includes dramatic sea cliffs, particularly along the northeastern coast of Molokai, which have been described as among the highest in the world. [4]

Hawaii’s landscape was formed by volcanic activity as the Pacific Plate moved over a hotspot beneath the central Pacific. [5] Although most volcanic activity is dormant, Mauna Loa, Kilauea, and the Lō‘ihi Seamount remain active or developing volcanic features. [5] The islands and seamounts of the Hawaiian chain are thought to have originated at least 70 million years ago. [5]

The climate and environment vary widely across the islands, with rainfall ranging from very low levels in some coastal areas to extremely high levels on windward slopes such as Waialeale. [4] The state has also experienced notable natural hazards, including major earthquakes and tsunamis, and water use, air quality, and noise levels have been measured and monitored as part of Hawaii’s environmental record. [1][4]

==History==
Hawaiʻi was first settled by Polynesians, who arrived around 1,500 years ago using the stars to navigate across the Pacific.[3] The Hawaiian Islands were later unified in 1810 by Kamehameha I, whose conquest established the Kingdom of Hawaiʻi.[3] In 1819, Liholiho, the son of Kamehameha I, helped end the traditional kapu system by publicly breaking the separation between men and women at a feast.[3] The first Christian missionaries arrived in Hawaiʻi in 1820, beginning a major period of religious and cultural change.[3][1] Lahaina served as the capital of the Hawaiian Kingdom from 1820 to 1845.[3] Sugar cultivation began to transform the islands’ economy in 1835, when the first sugar plantation opened on Kauaʻi.[3]  

European contact began in 1778, when Captain James Cook landed at Waimea Bay on Kauaʻi and named the archipelago the Sandwich Islands.[3] Cook was killed a year later at Kealakekua Bay on Hawaiʻi Island.[3] The islands subsequently became a strategic and commercial crossroads in the Pacific, and the nineteenth century saw increasing foreign influence through mission activity, trade, and labor migration.[1] Scholars have also emphasized Hawaiʻi’s multilingual and multicultural history, including the arrival of Chinese, Japanese, Russian, and other immigrant groups.[1]  

Geologically, the Hawaiian Islands were formed by volcanic activity over a hot spot in the Earth’s mantle, creating the Hawaiian-Emperor chain as the Pacific Plate moved northwest.[2][4] The Big Island of Hawaiʻi is the youngest island in the chain, while older islands and seamounts extend northwestward toward Kure Atoll and beyond.[2] Volcanic activity continues on Hawaiʻi Island, especially at Mauna Loa and Kīlauea, and the underwater Lōʻihi Seamount may eventually become a new island.[4]  

Hawaiʻi became the 50th state of the United States on August 21, 1959.[4] Since statehood, historians and cultural institutions have continued to document and preserve the islands’ complex past, from ancient settlement and kingdom-era politics to plantation society and modern statehood.[1][3]

==Demographics==
Hawaii had an estimated population of 1,441,553 in 2021, making it the 30th most populous U.S. state; this was an increase of 25,681 people, or 1.8%, from 2019. [1] By 2021, most residents lived on Oʻahu, which accounted for 69.4% of the state’s population, followed by Hawaiʻi Island, Maui County, and Kauaʻi County. [1] More recent Census estimates place the state population at 1,446,146 in 2024 and 1,432,820 in 2025. [5]

Hawaii is one of the most ethnically diverse states in the United States. [1] According to 2024 estimates, the population was 37.4% Asian alone, 25.1% White alone, 24.5% two or more races, 10.4% Native Hawaiian and Other Pacific Islander alone, 10.2% Hispanic or Latino, 2.2% Black alone, and 0.4% American Indian and Alaska Native alone. [5] In 2021, Japanese alone numbered 166,881 people and Chinese alone numbered 86,861, while Japanese alone or in combination and Chinese alone or in combination were 314,102 and 220,460, respectively. [1]

Immigrants make up a substantial share of Hawaii’s population. [1] In the 2021 American Community Survey, there were 270,345 immigrants in the state, or 18.8% of residents, the sixth-highest share among U.S. states. [1] That immigrant population was majority female, and Asians made up the largest ethnic group among immigrants. [1]

Native Hawaiians remain central to the state’s demographic profile. [4] One analysis described Native Hawaiians as about 6% of the islands’ population, or about 21% when people of mixed Native Hawaiian ancestry are included. [4] Another estimate placed the Native Hawaiian population at 683,000 in 1778, and projected continued growth in the decades ahead. [4]

The state’s population is relatively balanced by sex and has a median age that is slightly older than the U.S. average. [2][5] In 2024, Hawaii’s population was 49.7% male and 50.3% female, with a median age of 41.0 years. [2] For 2020–2024, the Census Bureau estimated that 20.3% of residents were under age 18 and 21.5% were 65 or older. [5]

==Economy==
Hawaii’s economy is heavily service-oriented and is tracked by the state’s Research & Economic Analysis Division, which publishes data, forecasts, and reports on key sectors such as tourism, labor, energy, population, and emerging industries.[1] The division also provides economic dashboards, data warehouses, and the official Census Data source for the state through the Hawaii State Data Center.[1]

Tourism remains one of Hawaii’s most important economic sectors, and state economic reports regularly monitor visitor arrivals, visitor expenditures, and daily visitor census levels.[3] In the fourth quarter of 2025, visitor arrivals declined, but visitor expenditures increased compared with the same period a year earlier.[3]

Labor market conditions have shown modest improvement, with the civilian labor force and non-agricultural wage and salary jobs both increasing in late 2025.[3] By February 2026, the statewide civilian labor force was estimated at 687,800, employment at 672,200, unemployment at 15,700, and the unemployment rate at 2.3 percent.[4]

Hawaii’s economy is more concentrated than the United States economy, with the top five industry groups accounting for a larger share of total GDP than the national top five industries.[2] In 2020, Hawaii’s real estate and rental and leasing, government, retail trade, construction, and professional, scientific, and technical services sectors formed the state’s top five industries by GDP share.[2] The state’s overall real GDP increased only slightly from 2010 to 2020, and the 2019-to-2020 decline was attributed to the economic effects of COVID-19.[2]

State reports also track tax revenues, building authorizations, inflation, and other indicators to provide a broader picture of current economic conditions.[3]

==Culture==
Hawaii’s culture is the product of layered influences from Native Hawaiian traditions and later arrivals from Asia, Europe, and North America.[1] After decades in which colonialism diminished Native Hawaiian practices, a cultural renaissance beginning in the 1970s helped revive the Hawaiian language and traditions such as voyaging canoes, tattooing, traditional music, kapa making, and hula.[1] Many residents still know at least some Hawaiian words and participate in customs such as giving leis, while the islands’ “Aloha Spirit” expresses a pattern of coexistence among diverse communities.[1]  

The traditions of immigrant groups have also become part of mainstream life in contemporary Hawaii, including celebrations such as Chinese New Year and the Japanese Bon festival.[1] At the same time, Native Hawaiian culture remains an important subject of historical and scholarly study, with research emphasizing its precontact development, social organization, and the effects of Western contact and colonization.[3][4] Some interpretations of Hawaiian social change have focused on the role of individual agency, especially Queen Kaʻahumanu’s use of political and symbolic power during a period of major transformation.[2]  

Hawaii is also home to major cultural institutions that preserve and interpret the islands’ heritage. The Bernice P. Bishop Museum in Honolulu is dedicated to the history, sciences, and cultures of the Pacific, and the Honolulu Academy of Arts holds major collections of Western and Asian art.[1] These institutions, along with active arts programs in the islands, contribute to the appreciation of Hawaii’s fine arts, history, traditions, and sciences.[1]  

In modern tourism, Hawaiian cultural traditions are often presented as living expressions of identity rather than as static heritage, with emphasis on spirituality, storytelling, community involvement, and respectful cultural engagement.[5]

==Tourism==
Tourism is one of the most important sectors of Hawaii’s economy and a major contributor to the state’s standard of living. [2] It also has wide-ranging effects on Hawaii’s physical infrastructure, natural resources, and social and cultural life. [2] State research has emphasized that tourism growth must be balanced with the need to sustain economic, environmental, and socio-cultural assets. [2]  

Visitor activity remains a central measure of the industry’s scale and performance. [5] In 2024, Hawai‘i Tourism reported total visitor spending of $20.68 billion, visitor arrivals of 9,657,607, and an average daily census of 236,784 visitors. [5] That same report estimated that tourism supported about 213,000 jobs and generated $2.40 billion in state tax revenue. [5]  

Air service is another key indicator of tourism demand. [5] Available airline seats to Hawai‘i increased slightly in the fourth quarter of 2025 compared with the same period in 2024, with both domestic and international capacity rising. [1] Even so, total available seats for 2025 as a whole were still below the previous year, reflecting declines in both domestic and international capacity. [1]  

Tourism in Hawaii has also been studied for its socio-cultural impacts. [4] Research on the subject has found that visitors may be more likely than residents to experience certain crimes, particularly larceny-theft, although the broader relationship between tourism and crime is complex and not always clear-cut. [4] More generally, tourism studies in Hawaii have examined how visitor growth affects community life, cultural values, social organization, and the distribution of costs and benefits across different groups. [4]  

The environmental and cultural effects of tourism are frequently linked to concerns about land use and urbanization. [3] Increased tourism can intensify development pressures, including hotel construction and associated infrastructure expansion, which may affect Hawaii’s landscapes and ecosystems. [3] At the same time, tourism can create cultural tensions when visitors are perceived as disrespecting local customs or contributing to cultural appropriation. [3]

==Health==
Hawaii’s health statistics are tracked through several state and federal data sources, including the Hawaii State Department of Health, the CDC, the U.S. Census Bureau, and the Hawaii Health Data Warehouse.[1][2][4] The Hawaii Health Survey is a continuous statewide household survey that collects information on health and socio-demographic conditions and is used by state agencies for planning, evaluation, and health research.[4] The survey includes core variables such as age, gender, income, ethnicity, education, household size, insurance status, poverty status, and self-reported physical and mental health.[4]

State health data also cover topics such as immunizations, infectious diseases, obesity, depression, crime, and school performance, with county-level breakdowns available for many indicators.[2] Hawaii Health Matters and related public health databases provide community snapshots and progress tracking for national health targets at the county level.[2] The Hawaii Health Data Warehouse compiles survey data from sources such as the Behavioral Risk Factor Surveillance System, the Pregnancy Risk Assessment Monitoring System, and the Youth Risk Behavior Survey.[2][4]

Public health reports indicate that health care demand in Hawaii is shaped by demographic change and by trends in conditions affecting adults and teenagers, including obesity and mental health concerns.[3] Studies of the state’s health care workforce note concerns about shortages, employment trends, and the factors that influence whether health care workers remain in Hawaii.[3] Historical and community-based scholarship on Native Hawaiian health emphasizes the long-term effects of population decline after Western contact and the importance of culturally informed care.[5]

==Education==
Hawaii’s public school system is organized as a single statewide district, so it is not evaluated in school finance reports that compare funding across multiple districts within a state.[1] In the Quality Counts report card, Hawaii ranked 22nd overall among the 50 states and the District of Columbia, with a grade of C, while its school finance category earned a B-minus and ranked 16th nationally.[1] The same report found that Hawaii scored a D-minus in current student performance, a C-minus for improvement over time, and identified poverty-based achievement gaps as a key area of concern.[1]

Education in Hawaii also includes a strong emphasis on Native Hawaiian and culturally based instruction.[4] The Kamehameha Schools’ Hawaiian Cultural Influences in Education study described statewide efforts to understand how culturally relevant education affects student engagement and outcomes, including the use of Hawaiian language, materials, and community resources in teaching.[4] More broadly, education research in Hawaii has focused on adapting curriculum and assessment to indigenous perspectives and on frameworks for Hawaiian indigenous education.[4]

The University of Hawaiʻi system is a major part of higher education in the state, with three universities, seven community colleges, and nine education centers across six islands.[5] Native Hawaiian students made up 23.3% of total enrollment in the UH system in fall 2017 and 21.3% of all degrees and certificates awarded in fiscal year 2017–2018.[5]

==Transportation==
Transportation accounts for about 55% of Hawaiʻi’s total energy use, with aviation and ground transportation making up the largest share.[5] Reducing petroleum dependence in this sector is a major part of the state’s long-term energy planning and economic resilience strategy.[5] Hawaiʻi’s transportation policy emphasis includes both shifting away from petroleum-fueled vehicles and improving system efficiency by reducing total transportation energy demand.[5]  

Because transportation electrification is expected to increase electricity demand, planning for transportation and the power grid must be coordinated across sectors.[5] Studies of Hawaiʻi’s clean energy goals identify reducing vehicle miles traveled, expanding electric-drive vehicles, and increasing the use of alternative fuels as key tactics for cutting petroleum consumption.[1] Over time, growth in population and vehicle ownership has increased total vehicle miles traveled in the state, even though miles traveled per vehicle have remained relatively stable.[1]  

Electric vehicles are seen as an important pathway for reducing greenhouse gas emissions in Hawaiʻi if they are powered by renewable electricity.[3] Research on EV adoption in the state has found that higher income, higher educational attainment, and greater charging infrastructure are associated with more registered electric vehicles.[3] Hawaiʻi’s electricity system, however, poses challenges for widespread EV integration because electricity prices are high and the grid has historically relied heavily on fossil fuels.[2]

==Governance==
Hawaiʻi was first settled by Polynesians, likely from the Marquesas, between about A.D. 300 and 600, and over the following centuries a distinct Native Hawaiian language, culture, and social system developed.[1] Native Hawaiian governance is described in historical scholarship as beginning with ruling chiefs long before the rise of the unified kingdom, with chiefly genealogies and oral traditions tracing lines of authority deep into the past.[1][3] By the late 18th century, the Hawaiian Islands were divided among various chiefs, until Kamehameha the Great unified them and established the Kingdom of Hawaiʻi.[2]

The Kingdom initially functioned as an absolute monarchy, but in 1840 King Kamehameha III signed the first written constitution, making Hawaiʻi a constitutional monarchy.[2] Successive constitutions gradually limited the political power of the monarch, including Queen Liliʻuokalani.[2] Traditional Hawaiian political structures were highly stratified by rank and genealogy, with high chiefs, lesser chiefs, and konohiki serving different governance roles within the islands’ land and social organization.[4]

During the nineteenth century, Hawaiian governance also shifted in ways that reduced Native Hawaiian political participation, as men became the primary political actors and voting was restricted by property, tax, and literacy requirements.[4] The arrival of missionaries and the adoption of Euro-American legal and political norms further reshaped the kingdom’s institutions.[4] After the overthrow period, Hawaiʻi became a republic on July 4, 1894, a United States territory on June 14, 1900, and the 50th U.S. state on August 21, 1959.[2]

Modern discussions of governance in Hawaiʻi often distinguish between the State of Hawaiʻi and Native Hawaiian self-governance.[3] Historical summaries produced for Native Hawaiian organizations emphasize that Native Hawaiians are the indigenous people of Hawaiʻi and that they exercised sovereignty over the islands for centuries, with forms of self-governance continuing into the present.[3][5] The Office of the Governor now oversees more than 160 boards and commissions, which provide a formal avenue for public input into state governance.[2]

==Hawaiian sovereignty movement==
The Hawaiian sovereignty movement emerged in the 1970s as a broad set of political, cultural, and legal efforts seeking self-governance, land stewardship, and, in some cases, restoration of an independent Hawaiian nation-state.[1][4][5] It developed in response to the 1893 overthrow of the Hawaiian Kingdom and the subsequent loss of Native Hawaiian political authority and international recognition.[3][5] Scholars and activists describe the movement as rooted in the historical sovereignty of the Hawaiian Kingdom and in unresolved questions of law, treaty history, and governance.[3][5]

The movement includes organizations and individuals with differing goals and strategies, ranging from cultural revitalization and reparations to international legal claims and full political independence.[2][3][4] Some groups have pursued decolonization and recognition in international forums, while others have emphasized land struggles, community activism, and the rebuilding of Native Hawaiian political institutions.[1][2] The movement gained momentum through campaigns and conflicts such as the Kalama Valley eviction, protests over Kahoʻolawe, and other land and eviction struggles that became touchstones for activism.[1] 

By the late 1980s and early 1990s, the sovereignty movement had become a significant force in Hawaiian politics and was increasingly treated as a legitimate political position among many Native Hawaiians.[4] It also became connected to broader indigenous rights movements in the United States and internationally, particularly through ideas of self-determination and indigenous jurisdiction over land and political identity.[4][5] Contemporary discussions continue to frame Hawaiian sovereignty as both a practical political question and a matter of historical justice for the Hawaiian people.[1][2][3]

==International sister relationships==
Hawaiʻi’s sister-state program is administered by the state Department of Business, Economic Development and Tourism, which says the partnerships are intended to strengthen Hawaiʻi’s international profile, encourage cooperative programs, and support economic development.[1] The program is governed by Hawaiʻi Revised Statutes chapter 229-2, which establishes the Hawaiʻi Sister-State and International Partnerships Commission to help the governor, legislature, and Office of International Affairs maintain long-term partnerships that benefit the state and its international partners.[1] DBEDT has stated that sister relationships should be based not only on friendship, cultural ties, and goodwill, but also on clear economic benefits, and that prospective relationships are evaluated through a formal application and review process.[1][2]  

The state has 19 sister relationships, with the earliest dating to 1981.[2] Hawaiʻi’s sister-state and related international partnerships include Guangdong (1985) and Hainan (1992) in China; Bali (2014) in Indonesia; Fukuoka (1981), Okinawa (1985), Hiroshima (1997), Ehime (2003), Hokkaido (2017), and Yamaguchi (2022) in Japan; Jeju (1986) in Korea; Rabat-Sale-Zemmour-Zaer (2011) in Morocco; Ilocos Sur (1985), Cebu (1996), Pangasinan (2002), Ilocos Norte (2005), and Isabela (2006) in the Philippines; the Azores Islands (1982) in Portugal; and Taiwan (1993).[1] Some of these relationships, including Hiroshima, Fukuoka, Okinawa, Taiwan, and Guangdong, have been described as especially active.[2]  

In addition to state-level ties, Hawaiʻi County has its own sister-city relationships, particularly in Japan, reflecting the state’s broader Indo-Pacific connections and the importance of migration and family heritage in these partnerships.[4]

[3] At the same time, tourism can create cultural tensions when visitors are perceived as disrespecting local customs or contributing to cultural appropriation. [3]

Hawaii’s health statistics are tracked through several state and federal data sources, including the Hawaii State Department of Health, the CDC, the U.S. Census Bureau, and the Hawaii Health Data Warehouse.[1][2][4] The Hawaii Health Survey is a continuous statewide household survey that collects information on health and socio-demographic conditions and is used by state agencies for planning, evaluation, and health research.[4] The survey includes core variables such as age, gender, income, ethnicity, education, household size, insurance status, poverty status, and self-reported physical and mental health.[4]

State health data also cover topics such as immunizations, infectious diseases, obesity, depression, crime, and school performance, with county-level breakdowns available for many indicators.[2] Hawaii Health Matters and related public health databases provide community snapshots and progress tracking for national health targets at the county level.[2] The Hawaii Health Data Warehouse compiles survey data from sources such as the Behavioral Risk Factor Surveillance System, the Pregnancy Risk Assessment Monitoring System, and the Youth Risk Behavior Survey.[2][4]

Public health reports indicate that health care demand in Hawaii is shaped by demographic change and by trends in conditions affecting adults and teenagers, including obesity and mental health concerns.[3] Studies of the state’s health care workforce note concerns about shortages, employment trends, and the factors that influence whether health care workers remain in Hawaii.[3] Historical and community-based scholarship on Native Hawaiian health emphasizes the long-term effects of population decline after Western contact and the importance of culturally informed care.[5]

Hawaii’s public school system is organized as a single statewide district, so it is not evaluated in school finance reports that compare funding across multiple districts within a state.[1] In the Quality Counts report card, Hawaii ranked 22nd overall among the 50 states and the District of Columbia, with a grade of C, while its school finance category earned a B-minus and ranked 16th nationally.[1] The same report found that Hawaii scored a D-minus in current student performance, a C-minus for improvement over time, and identified poverty-based achievement gaps as a key area of concern.[1]

Education in Hawaii also includes a strong emphasis on Native Hawaiian and culturally based instruction.[4] The Kamehameha Schools’ Hawaiian Cultural Influences in Education study described statewide efforts to understand how culturally relevant education affects student engagement and outcomes, including the use of Hawaiian language, materials, and community resources in teaching.[4] More broadly, education research in Hawaii has focused on adapting curriculum and assessment to indigenous perspectives and on frameworks for Hawaiian indigenous education.[4]

The University of Hawaiʻi system is a major part of higher education in the state, with three universities, seven community colleges, and nine education centers across six islands.[5] Native Hawaiian students made up 23.3% of total enrollment in the UH system in fall 2017 and 21.3% of all degrees and certificates awarded in fiscal year 2017–2018.[5]

Transportation accounts for about 55% of Hawaiʻi’s total energy use, with aviation and ground transportation making up the largest share.[5] Reducing petroleum dependence in this sector is a major part of the state’s long-term energy planning and economic resilience strategy.[5] Hawaiʻi’s transportation policy emphasis includes both shifting away from petroleum-fueled vehicles and improving system efficiency by reducing total transportation energy demand.[5]  

Because transportation electrification is expected to increase electricity demand, planning for transportation and the power grid must be coordinated across sectors.[5] Studies of Hawaiʻi’s clean energy goals identify reducing vehicle miles traveled, expanding electric-drive vehicles, and increasing the use of alternative fuels as key tactics for cutting petroleum consumption.[1] Over time, growth in population and vehicle ownership has increased total vehicle miles traveled in the state, even though miles traveled per vehicle have remained relatively stable.[1]  

Electric vehicles are seen as an important pathway for reducing greenhouse gas emissions in Hawaiʻi if they are powered by renewable electricity.[3] Research on EV adoption in the state has found that higher income, higher educational attainment, and greater charging infrastructure are associated with more registered electric vehicles.[3] Hawaiʻi’s electricity system, however, poses challenges for widespread EV integration because electricity prices are high and the grid has historically relied heavily on fossil fuels.[2]

Hawaiʻi was first settled by Polynesians, likely from the Marquesas, between about A.D. 300 and 600, and over the following centuries a distinct Native Hawaiian language, culture, and social system developed.[1] Native Hawaiian governance is described in historical scholarship as beginning with ruling chiefs long before the rise of the unified kingdom, with chiefly genealogies and oral traditions tracing lines of authority deep into the past.[1][3] By the late 18th century, the Hawaiian Islands were divided among various chiefs, until Kamehameha the Great unified them and established the Kingdom of Hawaiʻi.[2]

The Kingdom initially functioned as an absolute monarchy, but in 1840 King Kamehameha III signed the first written constitution, making Hawaiʻi a constitutional monarchy.[2] Successive constitutions gradually limited the political power of the monarch, including Queen Liliʻuokalani.[2] Traditional Hawaiian political structures were highly stratified by rank and genealogy, with high chiefs, lesser chiefs, and konohiki serving different governance roles within the islands’ land and social organization.[4]

During the nineteenth century, Hawaiian governance also shifted in ways that reduced Native Hawaiian political participation, as men became the primary political actors and voting was restricted by property, tax, and literacy requirements.[4] The arrival of missionaries and the adoption of Euro-American legal and political norms further reshaped the kingdom’s institutions.[4] After the overthrow period, Hawaiʻi became a republic on July 4, 1894, a United States territory on June 14, 1900, and the 50th U.S. state on August 21, 1959.[2]

Modern discussions of governance in Hawaiʻi often distinguish between the State of Hawaiʻi and Native Hawaiian self-governance.[3] Historical summaries produced for Native Hawaiian organizations emphasize that Native Hawaiians are the indigenous people of Hawaiʻi and that they exercised sovereignty over the islands for centuries, with forms of self-governance continuing into the present.[3][5] The Office of the Governor now oversees more than 160 boards and commissions, which provide a formal avenue for public input into state governance.[2]

The Hawaiian sovereignty movement emerged in the 1970s as a broad set of political, cultural, and legal efforts seeking self-governance, land stewardship, and, in some cases, restoration of an independent Hawaiian nation-state.[1][4][5] It developed in response to the 1893 overthrow of the Hawaiian Kingdom and the subsequent loss of Native Hawaiian political authority and international recognition.[3][5] Scholars and activists describe the movement as rooted in the historical sovereignty of the Hawaiian Kingdom and in unresolved questions of law, treaty history, and governance.[3][5]

The movement includes organizations and individuals with differing goals and strategies, ranging from cultural revitalization and reparations to international legal claims and full political independence.[2][3][4] Some groups have pursued decolonization and recognition in international forums, while others have emphasized land struggles, community activism, and the rebuilding of Native Hawaiian political institutions.[1][2] The movement gained momentum through campaigns and conflicts such as the Kalama Valley eviction, protests over Kahoʻolawe, and other land and eviction struggles that became touchstones for activism.[1] 

By the late 1980s and early 1990s, the sovereignty movement had become a significant force in Hawaiian politics and was increasingly treated as a legitimate political position among many Native Hawaiians.[4] It also became connected to broader indigenous rights movements in the United States and internationally, particularly through ideas of self-determination and indigenous jurisdiction over land and political identity.[4][5] Contemporary discussions continue to frame Hawaiian sovereignty as both a practical political question and a matter of historical justice for the Hawaiian people.[1][2][3]

Hawaiʻi’s sister-state program is administered by the state Department of Business, Economic Development and Tourism, which says the partnerships are intended to strengthen Hawaiʻi’s international profile, encourage cooperative programs, and support economic development.[1] The program is governed by Hawaiʻi Revised Statutes chapter 229-2, which establishes the Hawaiʻi Sister-State and International Partnerships Commission to help the governor, legislature, and Office of International Affairs maintain long-term partnerships that benefit the state and its international partners.[1] DBEDT has stated that sister relationships should be based not only on friendship, cultural ties, and goodwill, but also on clear economic benefits, and that prospective relationships are evaluated through a formal application and review process.[1][2]  

The state has 19 sister relationships, with the earliest dating to 1981.[2] Hawaiʻi’s sister-state and related international partnerships include Guangdong (1985) and Hainan (1992) in China; Bali (2014) in Indonesia; Fukuoka (1981), Okinawa (1985), Hiroshima (1997), Ehime (2003), Hokkaido (2017), and Yamaguchi (2022) in Japan; Jeju (1986) in Korea; Rabat-Sale-Zemmour-Zaer (2011) in Morocco; Ilocos Sur (1985), Cebu (1996), Pangasinan (2002), Ilocos Norte (2005), and Isabela (2006) in the Philippines; the Azores Islands (1982) in Portugal; and Taiwan (1993).[1] Some of these relationships, including Hiroshima, Fukuoka, Okinawa, Taiwan, and Guangdong, have been described as especially active.[2]  

In addition to state-level ties, Hawaiʻi County has its own sister-city relationships, particularly in Japan, reflecting the state’s broader Indo-Pacific connections and the importance of migration and family heritage in these partnerships.[4]
